unit05.title: Unit 5 - Data and Society
unit05.summary: Students learn how information is represented, collected, and used to solve problems, then build a simple recommendation engine using data they gather and analyze.
unit05.description: Data and Society emphasizes the importance of data in solving problems and highlights how computers support this process. Chapter 1 focuses on multiple systems for representing information in computers and the tradeoffs of each. Chapter 2 explores how collections of data are used to solve problems and how computers help automate this work. The unit concludes with a project in which students collect their own data and design a simple recommendation algorithm.

chapters.description: Representing Information: explore why representation matters, create and decode systems (text, images, numbers), combine representations, and practice basic data privacy and encryption.
chapters.goal01: Explain why data representation affects what we can do with information.
chapters.goal02: Use binary systems (ASCII, images, numbers) to encode and decode information.
chapters.goal03: Compare tradeoffs among different representation systems.
chapters.goal04: Describe basic concepts of data privacy and encryption.
chapters.goal05: Create a representation that communicates meaningful information.
chapters.big questions 01: How does the way information is represented affect our decisions and possibilities?
chapters.big questions 02: What are the systems computers use to represent text, images, and numbers?
chapters.big questions 03: How can we keep data secret and use it responsibly?

lessons.title: Representation Matters
lessons.overview: Explore what data is and how different representations of the same information can change the decisions we make.
lessons.question: How does data affect decisions we make every day?
lessons.content: Compare meal recommendations across multiple representations (pictures, recipes, menus, nutrition) and reflect on tradeoffs.
lessons.objective1: Define data and explain how representation influences conclusions.
lessons.objective2: Compare multiple representations of the same dataset.
lessons.objective3: Reflect on advantages and limitations of each representation.

lessons.title: Patterns and Representation
lessons.overview: Design a system that represents the alphabet using patterns of cards; exchange and decode messages to evaluate the system.
lessons.question: How can we create a system for representing information?
lessons.content: Brainstorm yes/no systems; create a pattern-based alphabet; test by encoding and decoding messages.
lessons.objective1: Create a consistent encoding system for letters using patterns.
lessons.objective2: Evaluate encodings for clarity, efficiency, and consistency.
lessons.objective3: Explain why shared, arbitrary conventions are necessary for communication.

lessons.title: ASCII and Binary Representation
lessons.overview: Connect prior work to real-world encodings; practice representing text with bits using ASCII.
lessons.question: What system do computers use to represent letters and words?
lessons.content: Learn bits and binary; encode/decode text using ASCII and discuss how computers store characters.
lessons.objective2: Encode and decode characters using ASCII.
lessons.objective3: Relate ASCII to broader ideas of digital representation.

lessons.title: Representing Images
lessons.overview: Investigate how pixel grids encode images with on/off values; try black-and-white pixelation challenges.
lessons.question: What system do computers use to represent images?
lessons.content: Use a pixelation model to represent images with bits; discuss resolution and limitations.
lessons.objective1: Explain pixels as units of image representation.
lessons.objective2: Encode simple images using binary on/off pixels.
lessons.objective3: Identify challenges in representing complex visuals with pixels.

lessons.title: Representing Numbers
lessons.overview: Use place-value cards to represent integers in binary, observing patterns and extending to a generic 4-bit system.
lessons.question: What system do computers use to represent numbers?
lessons.content: Turn bits on/off to form numbers; connect patterns to base-2 place value.
lessons.objective1: Represent integers using binary place value.
lessons.objective2: Relate bit patterns to decimal values.

lessons.title: Combining Representations
lessons.overview: Decode a record that mixes ASCII, numbers, and images; discuss why the ‘meaning’ of bits requires agreed structure.
lessons.question: How do computers tell the difference between binary codes for letters, numbers, or images?
lessons.content: Decode a pet record using structure; reflect on metadata, format, and context.
lessons.objective1: Interpret binary by using an agreed schema/format.
lessons.objective2: Combine multiple encodings to represent a single object.
lessons.objective3: Explain the role of metadata and structure in meaning-making.

lessons.title: Keeping Data Secret
lessons.overview: Explore why data needs protection and practice a simple binary encryption system to send secret messages.
lessons.question: How can we keep data secret and protect it from misuse?
lessons.content: Discuss risks; learn a punchcard-based binary encryption; encrypt/decrypt messages and reflect.
lessons.objective1: Explain reasons for protecting personal/sensitive data.
lessons.objective2: Apply a simple encryption method to encode/decode data.
lessons.objective3: Discuss benefits and limits of basic encryption approaches.

lessons.title: Project - Create a Representation
lessons.overview: Design and test a representation that communicates your ‘perfect day’ using ASCII, numbers, and images.
lessons.question: How can I represent complex information using binary systems?
lessons.content: Plan key information; encode with ASCII, numbers, images; exchange and decode with peers; reflect.
lessons.objective1: Plan and encode information using multiple binary systems.
lessons.objective2: Decode others’ representations and give feedback.
lessons.objective3: Reflect on tradeoffs when representing complex experiences digitally.

chapters.description: Solving Data Problems: collect, structure, interpret, and automate decisions with data; consider big data and machine learning; build a recommendation project.
chapters.goal01: Apply a problem-solving process to data problems.
chapters.goal02: Collect, clean, visualize, and interpret data to support decisions.
chapters.goal03: Design simple algorithms to automate parts of data decision-making.
chapters.goal04: Explain societal considerations of data collection, big data, and ML.
chapters.goal05: Create and present a data-driven recommendation.
chapters.big questions 01: How can we use data to solve problems in our community?
chapters.big questions 02: Which steps of the data process can computers automate—and which need humans?
chapters.big questions 03: How do big data and machine learning change what’s possible?

lessons.title: Problem Solving With Data
lessons.overview: Use the problem-solving process to investigate a civic decision and build an argument with evidence.
lessons.question: How can we use data to solve problems in our community?
lessons.content: Research options (e.g., city services); collect evidence; justify a recommendation; map steps to the process.
lessons.objective1: Apply Define→Prepare→Try→Reflect to a data problem.
lessons.objective2: Collect and cite data from credible sources.
lessons.objective3: Construct and present an evidence-based argument.

lessons.title: Structuring Data
lessons.overview: Clean and visualize results from a class survey; decide how to handle ‘other’ data and compare visualization choices.
lessons.question: How can we make it easier for computers to process data?
lessons.content: Organize and graph pizza-topping data; address messy entries; reflect on automation vs human judgment.
lessons.objective2: Handle irregular data and justify choices.
lessons.objective3: Identify steps that can be automated by a computer.

lessons.title: Interpreting Data
lessons.overview: Use cross-tabulation to examine relationships (e.g., cake vs icing) and consider how relationships inform decisions.
lessons.question: How can patterns in data help us make decisions?
lessons.content: Create a two-variable table/graph; analyze relationships; brainstorm problems this could help solve.
lessons.objective3: Identify limits of correlation for decision-making.

lessons.title: Making Decisions with Data
lessons.overview: Work through familiar scenarios to practice drawing conclusions from data—sometimes deciding to collect more.
lessons.question: How can patterns in data help make a decision?
lessons.content: Discuss group choices, justify with data, and reflect on differing conclusions from the same dataset.
lessons.objective2: Identify when additional data is necessary.
lessons.objective3: Reflect on how perspective influences interpretation.

lessons.title: Automating Data Decisions
lessons.overview: Design rules that a computer could use (e.g., vacation suggestions) and test on class data to compare to human choices.
lessons.question: How can computers help us make decisions about data?
lessons.content: Create and refine an algorithm; test against real data; discuss tradeoffs of automation.
lessons.objective1: Translate decision criteria into algorithmic rules.
lessons.objective2: Evaluate algorithm outputs versus human judgments.
lessons.objective3: Discuss benefits and drawbacks of automated decisions.

lessons.title: Problem Solving with Big Data
lessons.overview: Explore real services that use large-scale data; distinguish active, passive, and sensor data collection.
lessons.question: How is our data collected and why is it useful?
lessons.content: Analyze scenarios; watch a case-study video; classify data collection methods.
lessons.objective1: Describe how organizations collect and use data.
lessons.objective2: Differentiate active, passive, and sensor data.
lessons.objective3: Reflect on personal data and societal impacts.

lessons.title: Data and Machine Learning
lessons.overview: Use AI for Oceans to train and test a model, then choose your own labels to see how training data affects results.
lessons.question: How can machines "learn"?
lessons.content: Classify images as fish/not fish; expand training data; define new labels and test model behavior.
lessons.objective1: Describe the idea of training data and a model.
lessons.objective2: Explain how label choices and data affect outcomes.
lessons.objective3: Reflect on opportunities and limits of ML at this level.

lessons.title: Project - Make a Recommendation
lessons.overview: Plan and build a simple recommendation engine using class survey data; peer review and present.
lessons.question: How can I use data to make my own recommendations?
lessons.content: Choose a decision to support; design a survey; collect and interpret data; create and apply a recommendation algorithm; present.
lessons.objective1: Collect and analyze data to support a recommendation.
lessons.objective2: Define and implement a simple recommendation algorithm.
lessons.objective3: Iterate based on peer feedback and present findings.
